beats.narration_text: Polonium-212 decays in a fraction of a microsecond. Thorium-232 takes longer than the age of the universe. Both decay by the exact same mechanism.
The same exponential that governs alpha decay also sets your USB flash memory, powers the Sun, and images individual atoms with a scanning tunneling microscope.

props.headline: The same exponential that governs alpha decay also sets your USB…

beats.narration_text: One exponential controlling 24 orders of magnitude in half-life. And the same formula in a memory chip. How can one equation span so many scales and so many systems?

props.headline: One exponential controlling 24 orders of magnitude in half-life. And the…

card.headline: One exponential, 24 orders of magnitude.
card.dek: Alpha decay, flash memory, the Sun, the STM. How?

beats.narration_text: Classically, a particle with 5 MeV of energy cannot cross a 30 MeV barrier. It reflects off the wall. In quantum mechanics, the barrier has a finite width — and the wave function leaks through.
Inside the forbidden region the wave function doesn't oscillate — it decays exponentially. The thicker or higher the barrier, the more it decays before it can emerge on the far side.
The tunneling probability is e to the minus twice the Gamow integral — the integral of the momentum through the forbidden region divided by h-bar. Call it the Gamow factor.

Because the transmission sits inside an exponential, tiny changes in the barrier multiply into enormous changes in rate. Double the barrier width, and the probability doesn't halve — it drops by e to the minus a hundred.
A Coulomb barrier's width depends sharply on energy. A modest factor-of-two change in the alpha particle's energy changes the Gamow factor enough to swing the half-life by 24 orders of magnitude.

Every application is the same Gamow factor in different clothing. Stellar fusion: protons tunnel through the Coulomb barrier at energies far too low to clear it. Flash memory: electrons tunnel through a thin oxide to trap charge.

Illustrative: an electron with 1 eV of energy hits a 5 eV barrier that is 5 angstroms wide. The Gamow integral gives a decay constant of about 1 per angstrom. Over 5 angstroms, the tunneling probability is roughly e to the minus 10 — about 1 in 20,000.
One exponential governs every quantum tunneling process. Because the transmission lives inside an exponent, small changes in energy or width produce enormous changes in rate.

props.headline: One exponential governs every quantum tunneling process. Because the transmission lives…

card.headline: T ~ e^(-2 Gamow)
card.dek: Small changes in energy or width = enormous changes in rate.
